title: Rigid heddle doubleweave tube
subtitle: Weaving a seamless tubular cloth on a rigid heddle loom using a second rigid heddle or pick-up stick
excerpt: Doubleweave on a rigid heddle produces a seamless tube of cloth, twice as wide as the loom when opened out. The technique uses a pick-up stick to create the third shed needed to weave two separate cloth layers that connect at both selvedges. The result is useful for bags, narrow-width projects that require a wider cloth, or closed tubes for stuffed objects.
sourceNotes: Rigid heddle doubleweave is a well-documented advanced technique in rigid heddle instruction; the pick-up stick method described follows the approach standard in Jane Patrick and Stephanie Ashford-Brown's published instructions.

content.text: Rigid heddle doubleweave works by separating the warp into two interleaved layers: every odd-position thread weaves the upper cloth and every even-position thread weaves the lower cloth. The pick-up stick creates the
needed to weave each layer separately. By leaving both selvedges connected, the two
form a seamless tube. This entry covers the tube variant. For a double-width flat cloth, open one selvedge by weaving it as a plain selvedge turn rather than looping.

items.name: Rigid heddle loom with at least 30 cm weaving width
Pick-up stick (same width as or wider than the loom)
Weft yarn in same or contrasting colour

content.text: Warp the loom at normal sett for your yarn (for 8/2 cotton on a 15-dent heddle, one thread per slot and one per hole as standard). The key is how you thread: the slot threads will become one layer and the hole threads will become the other. This happens automatically on a rigid heddle because the heddle separates the warp into these two groups already.

With the heddle in the neutral (centre) position, slide the pick-up stick through the warp from the right, picking up every slot thread from below and passing under every hole thread. Push the stick back toward the back beam and leave it lying flat. This pick-up pattern creates the lower-layer shed when the stick is turned on edge.
Raise the heddle to the up position. Pass the shuttle through from right to left. This shed contains only the slot threads (upper layer). Beat gently.

Lower the heddle to the down position, then bring the pick-up stick forward and turn it on edge to open the lower-layer shed below the heddle shed. Pass the shuttle from left to right through the lower shed only, keeping the shuttle below the upper-layer warp threads. Beat gently. Return the pick-up stick to flat.

Repeat steps 1 and 2 throughout the piece. The tube forms automatically because the shuttle returns inside the tube on each pass; the selvedge threads at both edges connect the two layers. Check periodically that both layers are weaving separately by opening the tube with your fingers.

items.fix: Check that the shuttle tip enters and exits inside the tube, not wrapping around the outside edge. The weft should stay captured between the two layers.
items.cause: The shuttle passed through both layers at the edge instead of staying inside the tube
items.problem: The tube has fused layers at one selvedge

items.fix: Open the tube to confirm the layers are separate. For a visual distinction between layers, use two shuttles in different colours, one per layer.
items.cause: Both layers weave with the same shuttle. This is correct: separation is structural, not visual.
items.problem: The upper and lower layers look identical in colour when using one weft
